description:
A button lets your project notice when someone presses something.
Inside the button are tiny metal contacts. Pressing the cap joins the contacts together, closing the circuit.
Your projects can use a button to start actions, choose modes, confirm choices, or act like a simple controller.
parts: Cap: Press here; Contacts: Close circuit; Legs: Four pins; Body: Sits on board
code1:
from gpiozero import Button

button = Button(17)

pressed = button.is_pressed
code2:
from picozero import Button

button = Button(2)

pressed = button.is_pressed
note: Use button.is_pressed or button.when_pressed when your project needs to react to a press.
wiring: GPIO 17 -> one button leg | opposite leg -> GND | GP2 -> one button leg | opposite leg -> GND

description:
A buzzer lets your project make a simple sound.
Some buzzers just turn on and off, while others can make different tones.
Your projects can use buzzers for alarms, timers, games, tiny celebrations, and noisy mistakes.
parts: +: Signal; -: Ground; Hole: Sound comes out; Case: Holds disc
code1:
from gpiozero import Buzzer

buzzer = Buzzer(17)

buzzer.beep()
code2:
from picozero import Buzzer

buzzer = Buzzer(2)

buzzer.beep()
note: Use buzzer.on() , buzzer.off() , or buzzer.beep() after a Detect brick spots something.
wiring: + pin -> GPIO 17 | - pin -> GND | + pin -> GP2 | - pin -> GND

description:
A capacitive touch sensor lets your project detect a gentle finger touch.
It senses a tiny change in electricity around its touch pad, a bit like the screen on a phone or tablet.
Your projects can use touch to make hidden buttons, magic switches, musical pads, or secret controls.
parts: Pad: Touch area; OUT: Touch signal; VCC: Power; GND: Ground
code1:
from gpiozero import DigitalInputDevice

touch = DigitalInputDevice(
    17,
    pull_up=False
)

touched = touch.is_active
code2:
from picozero import TouchSensor

touch = TouchSensor(2)

touched = touch.is_touched
note: Use touch.is_active on Raspberry Pi or touch.is_touched on Pico when your project needs to react to touch.
wiring: VCC -> 3V3 | GND -> GND | OUT -> GPIO 17 | VCC -> 3V3 | GND -> GND | OUT -> GP2

description:
An LDR helps your project notice light and dark.
LDR stands for light-dependent resistor. Its reading changes when more or less light hits it.
Your projects can use an LDR to react to shadows, lamps, daylight, or secret hand waves.
parts: Face: Detects light; Track: Changes resistance; Leg 1: No polarity; Leg 2: No polarity
code1:
from gpiozero import LightSensor

ldr = LightSensor(17)

light = ldr.light_detected
code2:
from machine import ADC

ldr = ADC(26)

light_level = ldr.read_u16()
note: Use light or light_level when your project needs to react to light.
wiring: LDR and capacitor/resistor circuit -> GPIO 17 | other side -> 3V3/GND as drawn | Voltage divider signal -> ADC GP26 | ends -> 3V3 and GND

description:
An LDR is a light sensor that tells your project how bright or dark it is.
LDR stands for light dependent resistor. Its resistance changes when more or less light falls on the sensor face.
Your projects can use an LDR to react to sunshine, shadows, daylight, or darkness.
parts: Sensor face: Detects light; Track: Changes resistance; Legs: Connect to circuit
code1:
from gpiozero import LightSensor

ldr = LightSensor(18)

brightness = ldr.value
code2:
from machine import ADC, Pin

ldr = ADC(Pin(26))

brightness = ldr.read_u16()
note: Use brightness later in your Decide code to react to light or darkness. On Raspberry Pi, LightSensor uses an LDR and a capacitor. On Pico, use the LDR in a voltage divider with a fixed resistor.
wiring: 3V3 -> LDR -> GPIO 18 | GPIO 18 -> 1 uF capacitor -> GND | 3V3 -> LDR -> GP26 | GP26 -> 10k resistor -> GND

description:
An LED is a tiny light that your code can switch on, switch off, or blink.
LED stands for light-emitting diode. It glows when electricity flows the right way through it, so the long leg and short leg matter.
Your projects can use LEDs to show warnings, celebrate events, or tell people what is happening.
use2_title: Do not turn me off box
parts: Anode: Long leg; Cathode: Short leg; Lens: Glows here
code1:
from gpiozero import LED

led = LED(17)

led.on()
code2:
from picozero import LED

led = LED(2)

led.on()
note: Use led.on() , led.off() , or led.blink() after a Detect brick spots something.
wiring: GPIO 17 -> resistor -> LED anode | LED cathode -> GND | GP2 -> resistor -> LED anode | LED cathode -> GND

description:
An LED is a tiny light that your code can switch on, switch off, or blink.
LED stands for light-emitting diode. It glows when electricity flows the right way through it, so the long leg and short leg matter.
Your projects can use LEDs to show warnings, celebrate events, or tell people what is happening.
parts: Anode: Long leg; Cathode: Short leg; Lens: Glows here
code1:
from gpiozero import LED

led = LED(17)

led.on()
code2:
from picozero import LED

led = LED(2)

led.on()
note: Use led.on() , led.off() , or led.blink() later in your Do code.
wiring: GPIO 17 -> resistor -> LED anode | LED cathode -> GND | GP2 -> resistor -> LED anode | LED cathode -> GND

description:
A line sensor helps your project notice dark and light marks underneath it.
It shines infrared light down and checks how much bounces back from the surface.
Your projects can use line sensors for robots, edge finding, counters, and secret floor maps.
parts: Emitter: Sends IR; Receiver: Reads reflection; OUT: Signal pin; VCC/GND: Power pins
code1:
from gpiozero import LineSensor

sensor = LineSensor(17)

line_value = sensor.value
code2:
from picozero import DigitalInputDevice

sensor = DigitalInputDevice(2)

line_seen = sensor.is_active
note: Use line_value or line_seen when your project needs to react to a line.
wiring: VCC -> 3V3 | OUT -> GPIO 17 | GND -> GND | VCC -> 3V3 | OUT -> GP2 | GND -> GND

description:
A motor driver lets your code safely control a motor.
The driver handles the bigger motor power, while the computer sends small control signals.
Your projects can use motors to move wheels, fans, arms, signs, and tiny machines.
parts: Control: Signal pins; Motor: Terminals; Power: Motor supply; Driver: Switches current
code1:
from gpiozero import Motor

motor = Motor(forward=17, backward=18)

motor.forward()
code2:
from picozero import Motor

motor = Motor(forward=2, backward=3)

motor.forward()
note: Use motor.forward() , motor.backward() , or motor.stop() after a Detect brick spots something.
wiring: IN1 -> GPIO 17 | IN2 -> GPIO 18 | motor power -> driver VM/GND | IN1 -> GP2 | IN2 -> GP3 | motor power -> driver VM/GND

description:
NeoPixels are colourful lights that can be controlled one by one from a single data wire.
Each pixel remembers its own colour, so a strip can become a sign, meter, clock, or rainbow trail.
Your projects can use NeoPixels for badges, reactions, animations, and unnecessary sparkle.
parts: DIN: Data in; 5V: Power; GND: Ground; Pixel: RGB LED
code1:
from rpi_ws281x import PixelStrip, Color

pixels = PixelStrip(8, 18)
pixels.begin()

pixels.setPixelColor(0, Color(255, 0, 80))
pixels.show()
code2:
from machine import Pin
from neopixel import NeoPixel

pixels = NeoPixel(Pin(2), 8)

pixels[0] = (255, 0, 80)
pixels.write()
note: Use pixels.setPixelColor() and pixels.show() on Raspberry Pi, or pixels[0] and pixels.write() on Pico.
wiring: DIN -> GPIO 18 | 5V -> external 5V | GND -> shared GND | DIN -> GP2 | VCC -> 3V3 or 5V strip supply | GND -> shared GND

description:
A PIR sensor helps your project notice movement from people or warm things nearby.
PIR stands for passive infrared. It watches for changes in heat moving across its view.
Your projects can use PIR sensors for lights, alarms, counters, and dramatic entrances.
parts: VCC: Power; OUT: Motion signal; GND: Ground; Lens: Wide view
code1:
from gpiozero import MotionSensor

pir = MotionSensor(17)

motion = pir.motion_detected
code2:
from picozero import MotionSensor

pir = MotionSensor(2)

motion = pir.motion_detected
note: Use motion when your project needs to react to movement.
wiring: VCC -> 5V | OUT -> GPIO 17 | GND -> GND | VCC -> 3V3 or 5V module supply | OUT -> GP2 | GND -> GND

description:
A PIR sensor tells your project when something warm moves nearby.
PIR stands for passive infrared. It detects changes in infrared energy, so it is very good at noticing people moving through its view.
Your projects can use a PIR sensor for automatic lights, security alarms, or movement-triggered actions.
parts: Dome: Senses movement; Pins: VCC OUT GND; Adjusters: Time and range
code1:
from gpiozero import MotionSensor

pir = MotionSensor(4)

motion = pir.motion_detected
code2:
from machine import Pin

pir = Pin(2, Pin.IN)

motion = pir.value()
note: Use motion later in your Decide code to react when someone moves. Many PIR modules need a short warm-up time after power is connected before their readings settle.
wiring: 5V -> VCC | GPIO 4 GND | 5V -> VCC | GP2 GND

description:
A piezo buzzer is a small part that makes a buzz or beep when your code turns it on.
It changes electrical energy into vibration, which creates a sound you can hear without needing a screen or lights.
Your projects can use a buzzer for alarms, countdowns, warnings, or fun sound effects.
parts: Positive: Signal side; Negative: Ground side; Sound hole: Lets sound out
code1:
from gpiozero import Buzzer

buzzer = Buzzer(17)

buzzer.on()
code2:
from picozero import Buzzer

buzzer = Buzzer(2)

buzzer.on()
note: Use an active buzzer for the simplest setup. Try buzzer.on() , buzzer.off() , or buzzer.beep() later in your Do code.
wiring: GPIO 17 -> buzzer positive | buzzer negative -> GND | GP2 -> buzzer positive | buzzer negative -> GND

description:
A potentiometer is a knob your project can read.
Turning the shaft changes the voltage on the middle pin, giving your code a value from low to high.
Your projects can use a potentiometer for volume, speed, brightness, choices, and silly dials.
parts: End pin: One side; Wiper: Middle pin; End pin: Other side; Shaft: Turn here
code1:
from gpiozero import MCP3008

pot = MCP3008(channel=0)

level = pot.value
code2:
from picozero import Potentiometer

pot = Potentiometer(26)

level = pot.value
note: Use level when your project needs to react to the knob.
wiring: SIG -> ADC channel 0 | outer pins -> 3V3 and GND | SIG -> ADC GP26 | outer pins -> 3V3 and GND

description:
An RGB LED combines red, green, and blue lights in one part.
By changing the brightness of each colour, your code can mix lots of different colours.
Your projects can use RGB LEDs for moods, warnings, game states, and small light shows.
parts: Red: Red pin; Green: Green pin; Blue: Blue pin; Common: GND or 3V3
code1:
from gpiozero import RGBLED

led = RGBLED(red=17, green=27, blue=22)

led.color = (1, 0, 1)
code2:
from picozero import RGBLED

led = RGBLED(red=2, green=3, blue=4)

led.color = (1, 0, 1)
note: Use led.color after a Detect brick spots something.
wiring: Red -> GPIO 17 | Green -> GPIO 27 | Blue -> GPIO 22 | common -> GND | Red -> GP2 | Green -> GP3 | Blue -> GP4 | common -> GND

description:
A distance sensor measures how far away something is without touching it.
It works a bit like a bat, sending out sounds and listening for the echo bouncing back to understand how far away things are.
Your projects can use the distance to react when something moves closer or further away.
parts: VCC: Power; Trig: Sends pulse; Echo: Gets echo; GND: Ground
note: Use distance when your project needs to react to nearby things.
wiring: Trigger: GPIO 17 Echo: GPIO 18 | Trigger: GP2 Echo: GP3

description:
A speaker lets your project play tones and simple tunes.
Code changes the signal quickly to make the speaker vibrate at different pitches.
Your projects can use speakers for melodies, warnings, secret jingles, and dramatic noises.
parts: +: Signal; -: Ground; Cone: Vibrates; Magnet: Moves cone
code1:
from gpiozero import TonalBuzzer
from gpiozero.tones import Tone

speaker = TonalBuzzer(17)

speaker.play(Tone('A4'))
code2:
from picozero import Speaker

speaker = Speaker(2)

speaker.play('E4', duration=1)
note: Use speaker.play() after a Detect brick spots something.
wiring: + pin -> GPIO 17 | - pin -> GND | + pin -> GP2 | - pin -> GND

description:
A stepper motor moves in small controlled steps.
A driver board switches its coils in order, so code can choose direction and position more carefully.
Your projects can use steppers for dials, pointers, lifts, tiny plotters, and slow dramatic reveals.
parts: Shaft: Turns in steps; Coils: Inside motor; Wires: To driver; Mounts: Screw holes
code1:
from gpiozero import OutputDevice

coils = [OutputDevice(pin) for pin in (17, 18, 27, 22)]

coils[0].on()
code2:
from picozero import Stepper

stepper = Stepper((2, 3, 4, 5))

stepper.step(100)
note: Use coils[0].on() on Raspberry Pi or stepper.step(100) on Pico after a Detect brick spots something.
wiring: IN1-IN4 -> GPIO 17, 18, 27, 22 | driver power -> motor supply and GND | IN1-IN4 -> GP2, GP3, GP4, GP5 | driver power -> motor supply and GND

description:
A temperature sensor helps your project notice how warm or cold something is.
Many temperature sensors send a changing voltage or digital reading that code can turn into a temperature.
Your projects can use temperature to warn, log, cool, heat, or complain about soup.
parts: VCC: Power; DATA: Reading; GND: Ground; Probe: Sensing tip
code1:
from gpiozero import MCP3008

sensor = MCP3008(channel=0)

reading = sensor.value
code2:
from picozero import TemperatureSensor

def tmp36(voltage):
    return (voltage - 0.5) * 100

sensor = TemperatureSensor(26, conversion=tmp36)

temp = sensor.temp
note: Use reading or temp when your project needs to react to temperature.
wiring: Sensor OUT -> ADC channel 0 | sensor VCC -> 3V3 | sensor GND -> GND | Sensor OUT -> ADC GP26 | sensor VCC -> 3V3 | sensor GND -> GND

description:
A tilt switch lets your project notice when something tips or changes angle.
Inside, a tiny moving contact opens or closes the circuit when the switch tilts.
Your projects can use tilt to spot bumps, flips, lids opening, or things being picked up.
parts: Leg 1: Signal; Leg 2: Ground; Tube: Tilt body; Ball: Moves inside
code1:
from gpiozero import Button

tilt = Button(17)

tilted = tilt.is_pressed
code2:
from picozero import Switch

tilt = Switch(2)

tilted = tilt.is_closed
note: Use tilted when your project needs to react to being tipped.
wiring: One leg -> GPIO 17 | other leg -> GND | One leg -> GP2 | other leg -> GND

description:
A toggle switch stays in one position until someone flips it back.
That makes it useful for modes, power choices, and settings that should not disappear when you let go.
Your projects can use toggle switches for arming, choosing, locking, and secret control panels.
parts: COM: Common; NO: On side; NC: Off side; Lever: Flip here
code1:
from gpiozero import Button

switch = Button(17)

on = switch.is_pressed
code2:
from picozero import Switch

switch = Switch(2)

on = switch.is_closed
note: Use on when your project needs to react to the switch position.
wiring: COM -> GND | NO -> GPIO 17 | COM -> GND | NO -> GP2

description:
A distance sensor measures how far away something is without touching it.
It works a bit like a bat, sending out sounds and listening for the echo bouncing back to understand how far away things are.
Your projects can use the distance to react when something moves closer or further away.
parts: VCC: Power; Trig: Sends pulse; Echo: Gets echo; GND: Ground
note: Use distance later in your Decide code to make your project react.
wiring: Trigger: GPIO 17 Echo: GPIO 18 | Trigger: GP2 Echo: GP3

description:
An ultrasonic distance sensor measures how far away something is without touching it.
It sends out a tiny burst of sound and listens for the echo bouncing back.
Your projects can use distance to react when hands, walls, boxes, or mysterious objects move closer.
parts: VCC: Power; Trig: Send pulse; Echo: Hear echo; GND: Ground
note: Use distance when your project needs to react to nearby things.
wiring: VCC -> 3V3 or 5V module supply | Trig -> GPIO 17 | Echo -> GPIO 18 | GND -> GND | VCC -> 3V3 | Trig -> GP2 | Echo -> GP3 | GND -> GND

description:
An accelerometer senses movement, tilt, and how it is being held.
It measures force on three axes — x, y and z.
With an Arduino, an analog accelerometer is read on three analog pins.
parts: X, Y, Z: Three axes; Analog out: To analog pins; VCC: 3.3V power
note: An analog accelerometer (like the ADXL335) gives a voltage per axis that you read with analogRead() .
wiring: X → A0 | Y → A1 | Z → A2 | VCC → 3.3V, GND → GND

description:
A button or switch lets your project take input when someone presses or flips it.
When pressed it connects a pin to GND, which your code reads as a digital value.
With an Arduino, you use the internal pull-up resistor and read the pin as digital.
parts: Two legs: Pin and GND; Pull-up: Set in code; digitalRead(): 0 or 1
note: With INPUT_PULLUP the pin reads HIGH normally and LOW when pressed (button wired to GND).
wiring: pin 2 → one leg | other leg → GND

description:
A capacitive touch sensor reacts to your finger, just like a phone screen.
It senses the tiny electrical change your skin makes when you touch the pad.
With an Arduino, a touch module gives a digital HIGH when it is touched.
use2_do: Turn the lamp on or off
parts: Pad: Touch area; OUT: To a pin; Power: 3.3–5V
note: A TTP223 touch module outputs HIGH while the pad is touched — read it like a button.
wiring: OUT → pin 2 | VCC → 3.3V | GND → GND

description:
I²C is a way to connect modules like displays and sensors using just two shared wires.
Many devices can share the same two lines, each with its own address.
With an Arduino, the Wire library talks to I²C devices on A4 and A5.
parts: SDA: Data line (A4); SCL: Clock line (A5); Address: Per device
note: The Wire library uses A4 (SDA) and A5 (SCL) on an Uno. Each device has its own address.
wiring: SDA → A4 | SCL → A5 | VCC → power | GND → GND

description:
An LDR is a light sensor that tells your project how bright or dark it is.
LDR stands for light dependent resistor. Its resistance changes when more or less light falls on the sensor face.
With an Arduino, you read the LDR on an analog pin to get a light level.
parts: Sensor face: Detects light; Track: Changes resistance; Legs: Connect to circuit
note: analogRead() returns 0–1023. Wire the LDR with a 10kΩ resistor as a voltage divider into A0.
wiring: 5V → LDR → A0 | A0 → 10kΩ resistor → GND

description:
An LED is a tiny light that your code can switch on, switch off, or blink.
LED stands for light-emitting diode. It glows when electricity flows the right way through it, so the long leg and short leg matter.
With an Arduino, you connect an LED to a pin through a resistor and switch it on and off in your sketch.
parts: Anode: Long leg; Cathode: Short leg; Resistor: Protects LED
note: Use digitalWrite(led, HIGH) to turn the LED on and LOW to turn it off. Always put a 220 Ω resistor in series with the LED.
wiring: Pin 13 → resistor → LED + (long leg) | LED − (short leg) → GND

description:
A line sensor shines infrared light down and measures how much bounces back.
A dark line reflects little light and a pale surface reflects a lot, so the sensor can tell them apart.
With an Arduino, you read the sensor on a pin to follow lines or spot edges.
use1_decide: On the line or off it
parts: IR LED: Shines light; Detector: Reads reflection; OUT: To a pin
note: Most modules output digital (1 and 0 for line and no line). Analog versions use analogRead() . Adjust the sensitivity screw.
wiring: VCC → 5V | OUT → pin 2 | GND → GND

description:
A magnetometer measures magnetic fields, which lets the Arduino work as a compass.
It can give a heading in degrees where 0 means north.
With an Arduino, a compass module is read over I²C using a library.
parts: SDA: I²C data (A4); SCL: I²C clock (A5); VCC: 3.3V power; GND: Ground
note: Install a compass library. The module connects over I²C (SDA/SCL); getAzimuth() returns the heading.
wiring: SDA → A4 | SCL → A5 | VCC → 3.3V | GND → GND

description:
An LED matrix is a grid of LEDs you can light up to show numbers, letters and pictures.
A common module is an 8×8 grid driven by a MAX7219 chip.
With an Arduino, the LedControl library lights any LED in the grid.
use1_do: Show a happy or sad face
parts: DIN: Data; CLK: Clock; CS: Chip select; VCC: 5V
note: Install the LedControl library. lc(DIN, CLK, CS, devices) ; setLed() lights one LED by row and column.
wiring: DIN → pin 12 | CLK → pin 11 | CS → pin 10 | VCC → 5V, GND → GND

description:
A motor driver board lets your Arduino control motors that need more power than a pin can supply.
The Arduino sends low-power signals and the driver switches the larger motor current from its own supply.
You set direction with digital pins and speed with a PWM pin.
parts: IN1, IN2: Direction; ENA: Speed (PWM); Motor A/B: To the motors; VS / GND: Motor power
note: Power the motors from the driver board's own battery, not the Arduino. analogWrite() on ENA sets the speed.
wiring: pin 5 → IN1 | pin 6 → IN2 | pin 9 → ENA | battery → VS, GND → GND

description:
NeoPixels are addressable RGB LEDs you can chain together on a single data pin.
Each pixel can be a different colour and brightness.
With an Arduino, the Adafruit NeoPixel library sets each pixel's colour.
parts: DIN: Data pin; 5V: Power; GND: Ground
note: Install the Adafruit NeoPixel library. The first number is the pixel count, the second is the data pin.
wiring: DIN → pin 6 | 5V → 5V | GND → GND

description:
A piezo buzzer is a small part that makes a buzz or beep when your code turns it on.
It turns electrical energy into vibration, which makes a sound you can hear.
With an Arduino, you play tones through a pin using the tone() function.
parts: Positive: Signal side; Negative: Ground side; Sound hole: Lets sound out
note: tone(pin, frequency, duration) plays a note. Use noTone() to stop it early.
wiring: pin 8 → buzzer + | buzzer − → GND

description:
The Arduino's pins are how it connects to the outside world.
Digital pins read or write HIGH and LOW, analog pins (A0–A5) read voltages, and PWM pins (marked ~) can fake an analog output.
Power pins give 5V, 3.3V and GND for your components.
parts: Digital: HIGH or LOW; Analog: A0–A5; PWM: ~ pins; Power: 5V 3.3V GND
note: Digital pins use digitalRead/Write , analog pins use analogRead (0–1023), and ~ pins use analogWrite (0–255).
wiring: digital pins 0–13 | analog A0–A5 | power 5V, 3.3V, GND

description:
A PIR sensor tells your project when something warm moves nearby.
PIR stands for passive infrared. It detects changes in infrared energy, so it is good at noticing people moving.
With an Arduino, you read the sensor's output on a digital pin.
parts: Dome: Senses movement; Pins: VCC OUT GND; Adjusters: Time and range
note: digitalRead() is HIGH when movement is detected. Most PIR modules run on 5V and need a short warm-up.
wiring: VCC → 5V | OUT → pin 2 | GND → GND

description:
A potentiometer is a knob that changes its resistance as you turn it.
Wired as a voltage divider, it gives the Arduino a value that rises and falls as you turn it.
With an Arduino, you read it on an analog pin to get a number from 0 to 1023.
parts: Two ends: 5V and GND; Wiper: To analog pin; 0–1023: Value range
note: analogRead() gives 0 at one end and 1023 at the other.
wiring: 5V → one end | wiper → A0 | GND → other end

description:
A radio module lets two or more Arduinos talk to each other wirelessly.
Modules on the same channel and address can send and receive messages.
With an Arduino, the RF24 library sends and receives over SPI.
parts: CE: Pin 7; CSN: Pin 8; SPI: SCK MISO MOSI; 3.3V: Power
note: Install the RF24 library. The module uses SPI plus CE and CSN pins, and must run on 3.3V.
wiring: CE → pin 7 | CSN → pin 8 | SCK/MISO/MOSI → SPI pins | VCC → 3.3V, GND → GND

description:
An RGB LED is three coloured lights — red, green and blue — built into one part.
By mixing how bright each colour is, your code can make almost any colour.
With an Arduino, you connect the three colour legs to PWM pins and set each level.
parts: Red leg: Pin 9; Green leg: Pin 10; Blue leg: Pin 11; Common: 5V or GND
note: Use PWM pins (marked ~) and analogWrite() 0–255 per colour. Add a resistor on each leg.
wiring: pin 9 → R | pin 10 → G | pin 11 → B | common → GND or 5V

description:
A servo motor turns to an exact angle and holds it there.
It is great for arms, gates, dials, and steering.
With an Arduino, the Servo library moves it to an angle between 0 and 180 degrees.
parts: Signal: To a pin; Power: 5V; GND: Ground
note: Include the Servo library. write() takes an angle from 0 to 180 degrees.
wiring: signal → pin 9 | power → 5V | GND → GND

description:
A speaker turns electrical signals into sound you can hear.
On an Arduino you can drive a small speaker with the tone() function to play notes and tunes.
A speaker can play richer sound than a simple buzzer.
parts: Cone: Makes the sound; Terminals: Signal and GND; 8 Ω: Typical speaker
note: tone() plays square-wave notes through the speaker. For louder, cleaner sound, drive it through a small transistor or amplifier.
wiring: pin 8 → speaker + (via a resistor) | speaker − → GND

description:
SPI is a fast way to connect modules like screens, card readers and radios.
It uses separate data-in, data-out and clock lines, plus a select pin for each device.
With an Arduino, the SPI library handles the timing for you.
parts: SCK: Clock; MOSI: Data out; MISO: Data in; SS: Select
note: SPI uses pins 13 (SCK), 12 (MISO) and 11 (MOSI) on an Uno, plus a select pin for each device.
wiring: SCK → pin 13 | MISO → pin 12 | MOSI → pin 11 | SS → pin 10

description:
A stepper motor moves in small exact steps instead of spinning freely.
This lets your project turn something to a precise position, like a dial or a pointer.
With an Arduino, the Stepper library drives it through a board such as a ULN2003.
use2_do: Step the lift up or down
parts: Coils: Four windings; Driver: ULN2003 board; Steps: 2048 per turn; Power: Motor supply
note: A 28BYJ-48 motor has 2048 steps per turn. The ULN2003 driver board needs its own power supply.
wiring: pins 8, 9, 10, 11 → ULN2003 IN1–IN4 | battery → driver power

description:
A temperature sensor lets your project measure how warm or cold it is.
An analog sensor like the TMP36 gives a voltage that rises with temperature.
With an Arduino, you read it on an analog pin and convert the value to °C.
parts: Signal: To analog pin; Power: 5V; GND: Ground
note: A TMP36 gives 10 mV per °C with a 0.5V offset. Convert the analog reading to volts, then to °C.
wiring: signal → A0 | power → 5V | GND → GND

description:
A tilt switch is a small switch that turns on or off when it is tipped.
Inside is usually a tiny ball that rolls to make or break the connection.
With an Arduino, you read it on a digital pin, just like a button.
parts: Ball: Rolls inside; Contacts: Open or closed; Legs: Pin and GND
note: With INPUT_PULLUP the pin reads HIGH normally and LOW when the ball closes the contacts. Read it like a button.
wiring: pin 2 → one leg | other leg → GND

description:
A toggle or slide switch stays in the position you leave it — on or off.
Unlike a button, it does not spring back, so it remembers its setting.
With an Arduino, you read it on a digital pin to choose a mode or turn things on and off.
parts: Two positions: On and off; Stays put: No spring back; Legs: Pin and GND
note: A toggle switch holds its state, so the pin stays HIGH or LOW until you flip it. INPUT_PULLUP gives a clean reading.
wiring: pin 2 → one leg | other leg → GND

description:
UART is serial communication — sending text and data one bit at a time.
The Arduino uses it to talk to your computer and to modules like GPS or Bluetooth.
With an Arduino, the Serial library sends and receives over USB or pins 0 and 1.
parts: TX: Sends (pin 1); RX: Receives (pin 0); Serial: The library
note: Serial.begin() sets the speed. TX (pin 1) sends and RX (pin 0) receives — these are also used by the USB connection.
wiring: TX → RX of device | RX → TX of device | GND → GND

description:
A distance sensor measures how far away something is without touching it.
It works a bit like a bat, sending out sound and timing the echo that bounces back.
With an Arduino, you send a pulse on one pin and time the echo on another.
parts: VCC: Power; Trig: Sends pulse; Echo: Gets echo; GND: Ground
note: pulseIn() times the echo; multiply by the speed of sound to get the distance in cm.
wiring: Trig → pin 9 | Echo → pin 10 | VCC → 5V | GND → GND

description:
The accelerometer senses movement, tilt, and how the micro:bit is being held.
It measures force on three axes — x (left to right), y (forward and back), and z (up and down).
It can also spot gestures like shake, tilt, and free fall.
use2_do: Move the dot left or right
parts: x, y, z: Three axes; Gestures: Shake, tilt; milli-g: Force units
note: Use get_x() , get_y() and get_z() for raw force, or was_gesture('shake') for movements.
quick_reference: get_x() get_y() get_z(): Force on each axis; was_gesture('shake'): Detect a movement; Gestures: shake, tilt, face up, freefall; Built in: No wiring needed

description:
An external button or switch lets your project take input from anywhere, not just the two on-board buttons.
When pressed it connects a pin to GND (or 3V), which your code reads as a digital value.
With a micro:bit, you set the pin's pull resistor and read it as digital.
parts: Two legs: Pin and GND; Pull resistor: Set in code; read_digital(): 0 or 1
note: With a pull-up the pin reads 1 normally and 0 when pressed (button wired to GND). Set pin0.set_pull() before you call pin0.read_digital() .
wiring: P0 → one leg | other leg → GND | pull-up set in code

description:
The micro:bit has two buttons, A and B, that your code can react to when they are pressed.
They are built into the board, so there is nothing to wire up. Button A is on the left and Button B is on the right.
Your projects can use the buttons to choose options, control characters, or trigger actions.
parts: Button A: Left button; Button B: Right button; A + B: Press both together
note: Use is_pressed() to check if a button is held down right now, or was_pressed() to catch a press that already happened.
quick_reference: button_a, button_b: The two named buttons; is_pressed(): True while the button is held down; was_pressed(): True once after a press; Built in: No wiring needed — both buttons are on the board

description:
The micro:bit V2 has a touch-sensitive logo at the top that senses your finger.
Capacitive touch reacts to the tiny electrical change your skin makes, just like a phone screen.
Pins 0, 1, and 2 can also be set up to sense touch.
parts: Logo (V2): Touch sensor; Pins 0,1,2: Touch inputs; Capacitive: Senses skin
note: pin_logo.is_touched() works on the micro:bit V2. For pins, set the mode first, e.g. pin0.set_touch_mode(pin0.CAPACITIVE) .
quick_reference: pin_logo.is_touched(): V2 logo touch; pin0 pin1 pin2: Touch-capable pins; set_touch_mode(CAPACITIVE): Sense skin, not wires; Logo touch: V2 only

description:
The gold edge connector pins let your micro:bit talk to extra circuits and parts.
Some pins can read sensors, buttons, or dials. Pins can also turn things on and off.
Use the pins with LEDs, buzzers, motors, sensors, and your own strange inventions.
parts: P0 P1 P2: Large touch pins; 3V + GND: Power for circuits; Analog: Read changing values
note: Always connect GND too. Use write_digital() for on/off parts and read_analog() for sensors that give changing values.
quick_reference: pin0, pin1, pin2: The big edge connector pins; write_digital(1): Turn a pin on; read_analog(): Read a value from 0 to 1023; 3V + GND: Power and return path

description:
I2C lets your micro:bit talk to smart modules using two data wires.
Lots of sensors and small displays use I2C. Each device has an address.
Use I2C when a module needs more than simple on/off pin control.
parts: SDA: Data wire; SCL: Clock wire; Address: Device number
code1:
from microbit import *

devices = i2c.scan()
display.show(len(devices))

if devices:
    display.show(Image.YES)
code2:
let value = pins.i2cReadNumber(32, NumberFormat.UInt8BE)
basic.showNumber(value)
note: i2c.scan() is a handy first test in MicroPython. Check your module's address and voltage before sending commands.
quick_reference: i2c.scan(): Find connected I2C devices; SDA + SCL: The two I2C data wires; Address: The device you are talking to; 3V + GND: Power for the module

description:
An LDR is a light sensor that tells your project how bright or dark it is.
LDR stands for light dependent resistor. Its resistance changes when more or less light falls on the sensor face.
With a micro:bit, you read the LDR on an analog pin to get a light level.
parts: Sensor face: Detects light; Track: Changes resistance; Legs: Connect to circuit
code1:
from microbit import *

while True:
    brightness = pin0.read_analog()
    display.scroll(brightness)
note: pin0.read_analog() returns a value from 0 to 1023. Use brightness in your Decide code to react to light or dark.
wiring: 3V → LDR → P0 | P0 → 10k resistor → GND

description:
An LED is a tiny light that your code can switch on, switch off, or blink.
LED stands for light-emitting diode. It glows when electricity flows the right way through it, so the long leg and short leg matter.
With a micro:bit, you connect an LED to a pin and switch it on and off in your code.
parts: Anode: Long leg; Cathode: Short leg; Resistor: Protects LED
code1:
from microbit import *

pin0.write_digital(1)
sleep(500)
pin0.write_digital(0)
code2:
pins.digitalWritePin(DigitalPin.P0, 1)
basic.pause(500)
pins.digitalWritePin(DigitalPin.P0, 0)
note: Use pin0.write_digital(1) to turn the LED on and pin0.write_digital(0) to turn it off.
wiring: P0 → resistor → LED + (long leg) | LED − (short leg) → GND

description:
The micro:bit can sense light using its LED display as a light sensor.
It gives a light level from 0 (dark) up to 255 (bright).
Your projects can react to daylight, shadows, or a torch shining on the screen.
parts: LED display: Senses light; Range: 0 to 255; No extra parts: Built in
code1:
from microbit import *

while True:
    level = display.read_light_level()
    display.scroll(level)
note: Use display.read_light_level() to briefly use the LEDs to measure light and return a value from 0 to 255. Point the front of the micro:bit at the light.
quick_reference: read_light_level(): Light level 0 to 255; 0 = dark: 255 = bright; Uses the display: The 25 LEDs sense the light; Built in: No wiring needed

description:
A line sensor shines infrared light down and measures how much bounces back.
A dark line reflects little light and a pale surface reflects a lot, so the sensor can tell them apart.
With a micro:bit, you read the sensor on a pin to follow lines or spot edges.
use1_decide: On the line or off it
parts: IR LED: Shines light; Detector: Reads reflection; OUT: To a pin
code1:
from microbit import *

while True:
    on_line = pin0.read_digital()
    display.scroll(on_line)
note: Most modules output digital (1 and 0 for line and no line). Some have an analog output — read it with read_analog() instead. Adjust the module's sensitivity screw.
wiring: VCC → 3V | OUT → P0 | GND → GND

description:
The magnetometer measures magnetic fields, which lets the micro:bit work as a compass.
It can tell you the heading in degrees, where 0 means north.
It also senses strong magnets that are held nearby.
parts: Heading: 0 to 359°; Compass: 0 is north; Calibrate: Tilt to start
code1:
from microbit import *

compass.calibrate()
while True:
    heading = compass.heading()
    display.scroll(heading)
note: Call compass.calibrate() first and tilt the micro:bit to fill the screen. compass.heading() returns 0 to 359 degrees.
quick_reference: compass.heading(): Direction 0 to 359°; compass.calibrate(): Do this first; get_field_strength(): How strong the field is; 0° = north: Built in

description:
The micro:bit has a 5×5 grid of 25 red LEDs on the front, called the display.
Your code can show numbers, letters, pictures, or scroll messages across it.
Each LED can be set to a brightness from 0 (off) to 9 (brightest).
use1_do: Show a happy or sad face
parts: 25 LEDs: Red lights; 5 × 5 grid: Rows and columns; Brightness: 0 to 9
code1:
from microbit import *

display.show(Image.HEART)
sleep(1000)
display.scroll("Hi!")
code2:
basic.showIcon(IconNames.Heart)
basic.pause(1000)
basic.showString("Hi!")
note: Use set_pixel(x, y, b) to light one LED, where x and y are 0 to 4 and b is the brightness 0 to 9.
quick_reference: display.show(Image.HEART): Show a built-in picture; display.scroll("Hi!"): Scroll text across the grid; set_pixel(x, y, 0-9): Light a single LED; 25 LEDs: A 5 × 5 grid, brightness 0–9

description:
A motor driver board lets your micro:bit control motors that need more power than a pin can supply.
The micro:bit sends low-power signals and the driver switches the larger motor current from its own battery.
With a micro:bit, you set direction with digital pins and speed with a PWM (analog) pin.
parts: IN1, IN2: Direction; Motor A/B: To the motors; VM: Motor power in; GND: Shared ground
code1:
from microbit import *

# motor forward at about 70% speed
pin0.write_analog(700)
pin1.write_digital(0)
note: Power the motors from the driver board's own battery, never from the micro:bit. Many driver boards have a MakeCode extension with ready-made motor blocks. Use pin0.write_analog() to set the motor speed.
wiring: P0 → IN1 (PWM) | P1 → IN2 | battery → VM | GND → GND

description:
NeoPixels are colourful LEDs that your micro:bit can control one at a time.
A strip or ring uses one data pin, plus power and ground.
Use them for status lights, glowing signs, rainbow effects, or wearable sparkle.
parts: DIN: Data input pin; RGB: Red, green, blue; Power: Check voltage first
code1:
from microbit import *
import neopixel

strip = neopixel.NeoPixel(pin0, 8)

strip[0] = (255, 0, 0)
strip[1] = (0, 255, 0)
strip[2] = (0, 0, 255)
strip.show()
code2:
let strip = neopixel.create(DigitalPin.P0, 8, NeoPixelMode.RGB)

strip.setPixelColor(0, neopixel.colors(NeoPixelColors.Red))
strip.setPixelColor(1, neopixel.colors(NeoPixelColors.Green))
strip.setPixelColor(2, neopixel.colors(NeoPixelColors.Blue))
strip.show()
note: In MakeCode, add the NeoPixel extension first. NeoPixels often need more power than the micro:bit can safely provide, so check the strip and share GND .
quick_reference: NeoPixel(pin0, 8): 8 pixels on pin 0; (255, 0, 0): Red, green, blue values; strip.show(): Send colours to the LEDs; GND: Must be shared with the strip

description:
A piezo buzzer is a small part that makes a buzz or beep when your code turns it on.
It turns electrical energy into vibration, which makes a sound you can hear without a screen or lights.
With a micro:bit, you play tones through a pin connected to the buzzer.
parts: Positive: Signal side; Negative: Ground side; Sound hole: Lets sound out
code1:
import music
from microbit import *

music.pitch(440, 500, pin0)
code2:
pins.analogSetPitchPin(AnalogPin.P0)
music.playTone(262, music.beat(BeatFraction.Whole))
note: Set the pitch pin to P0 so tones play through the external buzzer, then use music.pitch() or music.play() for tunes.
wiring: P0 → buzzer + | buzzer − → GND

description:
A PIR sensor tells your project when something warm moves nearby.
PIR stands for passive infrared. It detects changes in infrared energy, so it is good at noticing people moving.
With a micro:bit, you read the sensor's output on a digital pin.
parts: Dome: Senses movement; Pins: VCC OUT GND; Adjusters: Time and range
note: pin0.read_digital() is 1 when movement is detected. Many PIR modules need 5V power and a short warm-up after switching on.
wiring: VCC → 3V | OUT → P0 | GND → GND

description:
A potentiometer is a knob that changes its resistance as you turn it.
Wired as a voltage divider, it gives the micro:bit a value that rises and falls as you turn it.
With a micro:bit, you read it on an analog pin to get a number from 0 to 1023.
parts: Two ends: 3V and GND; Wiper: To analog pin; Range: 0 to 1023
code1:
from microbit import *

while True:
    value = pin0.read_analog()
    display.scroll(value)
note: pin0.read_analog() returns 0 at one end and 1023 at the other. Only P0, P1 and P2 can read analog values.
wiring: 3V → one end | wiper → P0 | GND → other end

description:
The radio lets two or more micro:bits talk to each other wirelessly.
micro:bits set to the same group number can send and receive short messages.
Your projects can build chat tools, remote controls, or multiplayer games.
parts: Group: Channel 0–255; send(): Transmit; receive(): Listen
code1:
import radio
from microbit import *

radio.on()
radio.config(group=23)

while True:
    if button_a.was_pressed():
        radio.send("hello")
    msg = radio.receive()
note: All micro:bits must use the same group number to hear each other. Call radio.on() before sending or receiving.
quick_reference: radio.on(): Turn the radio on; radio.config(group=23): Same group to connect; send() / receive(): Talk wirelessly; Built in: No wiring needed

description:
An RGB LED is three coloured lights — red, green and blue — built into one part.
By mixing how bright each colour is, your code can make almost any colour.
With a micro:bit, you connect the three colour legs to the three PWM pins and set each level.
parts: Red leg: P0; Green leg: P1; Blue leg: P2; Common: 3V or GND
code1:
from microbit import *

pin0.write_analog(1023)  # red
pin1.write_analog(400)   # green
pin2.write_analog(0)     # blue
code2:
pins.analogWritePin(AnalogPin.P0, 1023)
pins.analogWritePin(AnalogPin.P1, 400)
pins.analogWritePin(AnalogPin.P2, 0)
note: Each colour uses a PWM pin (P0, P1, P2) — that is all three of the micro:bit's analog outputs. Add a resistor on each leg; for a common-anode LED, pin0.write_analog() with 0 is brightest.
wiring: P0 → R | P1 → G | P2 → B | common → GND or 3V

description:
A servo motor turns to a position you choose, instead of spinning forever.
Your micro:bit sends a timed signal to the servo using one pin.
Use servos for robot arms, pointers, locks, gates, flaps, and wobbly creatures.
parts: Signal: One control pin; Angle: Usually 0 to 180; Power: Often external
code1:
from microbit import *

pin0.set_analog_period(20)

pin0.write_analog(51)
sleep(1000)
pin0.write_analog(77)
sleep(1000)
pin0.write_analog(102)
code2:
pins.servoWritePin(AnalogPin.P0, 0)
basic.pause(1000)
pins.servoWritePin(AnalogPin.P0, 90)
basic.pause(1000)
pins.servoWritePin(AnalogPin.P0, 180)
note: Servos can pull more current than a micro:bit pin can provide. Use a suitable power supply for the servo and connect the servo GND to the micro:bit GND .
quick_reference: servoWritePin: MakeCode servo angle command; set_analog_period(20): 20 ms servo pulse timing; Signal wire: Connects to a pin; Shared GND: Needed with external power

description:
The micro:bit V2 has a tiny speaker built in, so it can make sounds and play tunes with no extra parts.
Your code can play built-in melodies, single notes, or fun sound effects.
Your projects can use sound for alarms, music, games, or feedback.
parts: Speaker (V2): Built-in buzzer; music: Tunes and notes; Pin 0: Headphones on V1
code1:
import music
from microbit import *

music.play(music.POWER_UP)
sleep(500)
music.pitch(440, 500)
code2:
music.playTone(262, music.beat(BeatFraction.Whole))
music.startMelody(music.builtInMelody(Melodies.PowerUp), MelodyOptions.Once)
note: The built-in speaker is on the micro:bit V2. On a V1, connect a buzzer or headphones to pin0 and GND .
quick_reference: import music: Sounds and tunes; music.play(music.POWER_UP): Play a built-in melody; music.pitch(440, 500): A note for a number of ms; Speaker: Built in on V2, pin 0 on V1

description:
SPI is a fast way for your micro:bit to send data to modules.
It uses a clock wire, data wires, and usually a chip select pin.
Use SPI with displays, memory modules, LED drivers, and other speedy parts.
parts: SCK: Clock wire; MOSI/MISO: Data wires; CS: Choose device
code1:
from microbit import *

spi.init(baudrate=1000000)

pin0.write_digital(0)
spi.write(b'\x00')
pin0.write_digital(1)
code2:
pins.spiPins(DigitalPin.P15, DigitalPin.P14, DigitalPin.P13)

pins.digitalWritePin(DigitalPin.P0, 0)
pins.spiWrite(0)
pins.digitalWritePin(DigitalPin.P0, 1)
note: SPI modules often need their own setup rules. Match the module's pins, speed, and chip select wiring before sending data.
quick_reference: spi.init(): Set up SPI in MicroPython; spi.write(): Send bytes to a module; SCK / MOSI / MISO: Clock and data wires; CS: Chip select pin

description:
A stepper motor moves in small exact steps instead of spinning freely.
This lets your project turn something to a precise position, like a dial or a pointer.
With a micro:bit, you drive it through a small driver board such as a ULN2003.
use2_do: Step the lift up or down
parts: Coils: Four windings; Driver: ULN2003 board; Steps: Exact moves; Power: Motor supply
note: Step through the four coils in order to turn the motor. A ULN2003 driver board and its own power supply do the heavy lifting.
wiring: P0–P2, P8 → ULN2003 IN1–IN4 | driver → motor | battery → driver power

description:
The micro:bit can measure temperature in degrees Celsius using a sensor on its main chip.
It reads the temperature of the board, which is usually close to the room temperature.
Your projects can react to warm or cold, log readings, or warn about heat.
parts: °C: Degrees Celsius; On the chip: Senses board heat; No extra parts: Built in
code1:
from microbit import *

while True:
    temp = temperature()
    display.scroll(temp)
note: temperature() returns the chip temperature in °C. It can read a little high because it senses the board, not the air.
quick_reference: temperature(): Degrees Celsius; On the chip: Reads the board's heat; Reads a bit high: Board is warmer than air; Built in: No wiring needed

description:
A tilt switch is a small switch that turns on or off when it is tipped.
Inside is usually a tiny ball that rolls to make or break the connection.
With a micro:bit, you read it on a pin, just like a button.
parts: Ball: Rolls inside; Contacts: Open or closed; Legs: To a pin and GND
note: A tilt switch reads like a button: set a pull-up and watch the contact open and close as it tips.
wiring: P0 → one leg | other leg → GND | pull-up set in code

description:
A toggle or slide switch stays in the position you leave it — on or off.
Unlike a button, it does not spring back, so it remembers its setting.
With a micro:bit, you read it on a pin to choose a mode or turn things on and off.
parts: Two positions: On and off; Stays put: No spring back; Legs: To a pin and GND
note: A toggle switch holds its state, so the pin stays HIGH or LOW until you flip it. Set a pull-up and read it as digital.
wiring: P0 → one leg | other leg → GND | pull-up set in code

description:
UART is a simple serial link that sends messages one bit after another.
Your micro:bit can use a TX pin to send and an RX pin to listen.
Use UART with GPS receivers, other boards, serial modules, or computer messages.
parts: TX: Sends data; RX: Receives data; Baud: Message speed
code1:
from microbit import *

uart.init(baudrate=9600, tx=pin0, rx=pin1)
uart.write('hello\n')

if uart.any():
    message = uart.readline()
    display.show(Image.YES)
code2:
serial.redirect(
    SerialPin.P0,
    SerialPin.P1,
    BaudRate.BaudRate9600
)
serial.writeLine("hello")
note: For many serial modules, connect the module's TX to the micro:bit's RX , and the module's RX to the micro:bit's TX . Share GND too.
quick_reference: uart.init(..., tx, rx): Set speed and pins; uart.write(): Send text or bytes; uart.readline(): Read a line of data; TX + RX: Transmit and receive pins

description:
A distance sensor measures how far away something is without touching it.
It works a bit like a bat, sending out sound and listening for the echo to bounce back.
With a micro:bit, the MakeCode sonar extension reads the distance from a trigger and an echo pin.
parts: VCC: Power; Trig: Sends pulse; Echo: Gets echo; GND: Ground
code1:
from microbit import *
from machine import time_pulse_us

pin0.write_digital(0)
pin0.write_digital(1)
pin0.write_digital(0)
duration = time_pulse_us(pin1, 1)
distance = duration / 58
note: In MakeCode, add the sonar extension first. Use distance in your Decide code to make the project react.
wiring: Trig → P0 | Echo → P1 | VCC → 3V | GND → GND
